blocks:
Ayahs

إِذَا زُلْزِلَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ زِلْزَالَهَا (١)
When the earth is rocked with a terrible quake (1)
وَأَخْرَجَتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ أَثْقَالَهَا (٢)
and discharges its burdens, (2)
وَقَالَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مَا لَهَا (٣)
and man says, ‘What is the matter with her?’ (3)
يَوْمَئِذٍ تُحَدِّثُ أَخْبَارَهَا (٤)
On that day she will relate her chronicles (4)
بِأَنَّ رَبَّكَ أَوْحَىٰ لَهَا (٥)
for her Lord will have inspired her. (5)
يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصْدُرُ ٱلنَّاسُ أَشْتَاتًا لِّيُرَوْا۟ أَعْمَـٰلَهُمْ (٦)
On that day, mankind will issue forth in various groups to be shown their deeds. (6)
فَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْرًا يَرَهُۥ (٧)
So whoever does an atom’s weight of good will see it, (7)
وَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِثْقَالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُۥ (٨)
and whoever does an atom’s weight of evil will see it. (8)

Content and Virtue of Surah "Az-Zalzalah"

There is a discussion among commentators as to whether this Surah was revealed in "Mecca" or Medina. Many consider it Medinan, while some believe it was revealed in "Mecca." The tone of its verses, which speak of the "Resurrection" and "Ashrat al-Sa'ah" (the signs of the occurrence of the Resurrection), is more similar to Meccan verses. However, a hadith states that when this verse was revealed: فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ ..., Abu Sa'id al-Khudri asked the Holy Prophet ﷺ a question about it, and we know that Abu Sa'id joined the Muslims in Medina [^1]. Nevertheless, whether this Surah is Medinan or Meccan does not affect its concepts and interpretation.

In any case, this Surah primarily revolves around three axes:

First, it discusses "Ashrat al-Sa'ah" and the signs of the occurrence of the Resurrection, followed by the testimony of the earth regarding all human deeds.

In the third section, it speaks of the division of people into two groups, "the righteous" and "the wicked," and each person attaining their own deeds.

Regarding the virtue of this Surah, significant expressions have appeared in Islamic narrations. Among them, we read in a hadith from the Holy Prophet ﷺ:

“Whoever recites it, it is as if he has recited Surah Al-Baqarah, and he is given a reward equal to one who has recited a quarter of the Quran.” [^1]

And in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read that he said: "Never tire of reciting Surah 'Idha Zulzilat al-Ard,' for whoever recites it in their supererogatory prayers (Nafilah) will never be afflicted by an earthquake, will not die by one, and will not be afflicted by lightning or any worldly calamity until the time of death." ^2

[^1]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 30, p. 208. [^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 524. ^2: Usul al-Kafi (as cited in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 347).

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ

إِذا زُلْزِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ زِلْزالَها (1) وَ أَخْرَجَتِ الْأَرْضُ أَثْقالَها (2) وَ قالَ الْإِنْسانُ ما لَها (3) يَوْمَئِذٍ تُحَدِّثُ أَخْبارَها (4)

بِأَنَّ رَبَّكَ أَوْحى‌ لَها (5) يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصْدُرُ النَّاسُ أَشْتاتاً لِيُرَوْا أَعْمالَهُمْ (6) فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْراً يَرَهُ (7) وَ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ (8)

Commentary

That Day When Man Sees All His Deeds!

As mentioned in the description of the Surah's content, the beginning of this Surah is accompanied by the description of some of the terrifying and horrific events of the end of this world and the start of the Resurrection.

First, He says: "When the earth is shaken with its [final] earthquake" (إِذا زُلْزِلَتِ الْأَرْضُ زِلْزالَها) [^1]

[^1]: "Idha" (When) here is conditional. Regarding what the "Jaza" (result of the condition) is, several possibilities have been suggested: some consider it to be يَوْمَئِذٍ تُحَدِّثُ أَخْبارَها, some يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصْدُرُ النَّاسُ أَشْتاتاً, and some consider the result to be omitted, such that when people asked "When is the Hour?" (When will the Resurrection occur?), He replied: When that great earthquake occurs—meaning at that time, the Resurrection takes place.

And it is so overturned that "And the earth discharges its burdens" (وَ أَخْرَجَتِ الْأَرْضُ أَثْقالَها).

The expression "Zilzalaha" (its earthquake) either refers to the fact that on that day the entire globe will shake (unlike ordinary earthquakes which are all local and temporary), or it refers to the promised earthquake, namely the earthquake of the Resurrection ^1. Regarding what is meant by "Athqal" (heavy burdens), commentators have mentioned several interpretations: some have said it refers to human beings who will be cast out from within the graves by the earthquake of the Resurrection, similar to what appears in Surah Al-Inshiqaq, verse 4: "And throws out what is within it and becomes empty."

Others have said: it pours out the treasures within itself, becoming a source of regret for the heedless worldly-minded ^3. There is also the possibility that it refers to the ejection of heavy and molten materials from within the earth, a small amount of which usually comes out during volcanoes and earthquakes; at the end of the world, whatever is inside the earth will be thrown out following that massive earthquake.

[^1]: In the first case, the "Idhafah" (possessive construction) conveys a general meaning, and in the second case, it refers to a specific, known event.

^3: "Athqal" is the plural of "Thiql" (rhyming with fikr) meaning "burden." Some have considered it the plural of "Thaqal" (rhyming with amal) meaning household goods or traveler's gear, but the first meaning seems more appropriate.

The first interpretation seems more fitting, although a combination of these interpretations is also not unlikely.

In any case, on that day, man will be severely terrified by seeing this unprecedented scene and will say: "What is [wrong] with it—that the earth has shaken like this and cast out what it held within?" (وَ قالَ الْإِنْسانُ ما لَها). Although some have interpreted "Man" here specifically as the disbelievers who had doubts about the issue of the Return and Resurrection, it appears that "Man" here has a broad meaning that includes everyone, as the wonder at the state of the earth on that day is not exclusive to disbelievers.

Is this wonder and the resulting question related to the "First Trumpet" or the "Second"?

It appears to be the first trumpet, which is the trumpet of the end of the world, because the great earthquake occurs at the end of the world.

The possibility has also been raised that it refers to the trumpet of the Resurrection, the reviving of the dead, and their being cast out from within the earth, as the subsequent verses are all related to the second trumpet.

However, since the verses of the Quran frequently mention the events of these two trumpets together, the first interpretation—considering the description of the terrifying earthquake at the end of the world—seems more appropriate. In this case, "Athqal" (burdens) of the earth would mean the minerals, treasures, and molten materials within it.

And more importantly: "That Day, it will report its news" (يَوْمَئِذٍ تُحَدِّثُ أَخْبارَها).

Whatever good and evil, and deeds of virtue or vice, have occurred upon the surface of the earth, it will reveal them all. One of the most important witnesses to human actions on that day is this very earth upon which we perform our deeds, and which is a witness and observer over us.

In a hadith from the Holy Prophet ﷺ, we read that he asked: "Do you know what its news is?" They said: "Allah and His Messenger know best." He said: "Its news is that it will testify against every servant and nation regarding what they did upon its back. It will say: 'He did such and such, on such and such a day.' This is its reporting of news!" [^1]

In another hadith from the Holy Prophet ﷺ, we read: "Be diligent in your Wudu (ablution), and the best of your deeds is Salah (prayer). So be mindful of the earth, for it is your mother, and there is no one who performs a good or evil deed upon it except that it will report it!" ^2

It is narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri that he used to say: "Whenever you are in the desert, raise your voice with the Adhan, for I heard the Messenger of Allah ﷺ say: 'No Jinn, human, or stone hears it except that it will testify for him (on the Day of Resurrection).' ^3

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 649. ^2: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 526.

Will the earth truly open its tongue and speak by the command of Allah? Or does it mean the manifestation of the effects of human deeds upon the surface of the earth? For we know that every action a person performs inevitably leaves traces around them, even if they are not perceptible to us today—just like the fingerprints of a friend or enemy left on a door handle. On that day, all these traces will become manifest, and the earth's "speaking" is nothing other than this great manifestation. Just as we sometimes say to a drowsy person, "Your eyes say that you did not sleep last night," meaning the effects of sleeplessness are visible in them.

In any case, this is not a strange matter, for even today, with the progress of human science and knowledge, tools and devices have been invented that can record a person's voice anywhere and at any moment, or take photos of a person and their actions, to be presented as definitive evidence in court in a way that cannot be denied.

If in the past some were surprised by the testimony of the earth, today a thin tape or a recording device disguised as a button on clothing can reveal many matters.

Interestingly, in a hadith from Ali عليه السلام, we read that he said: "Pray in different parts of the mosques, for every piece of land will testify for the one who prayed upon it on the Day of Resurrection." [^1]

And in another hadith, it is mentioned that when the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, finished distributing the public treasury (Bayt al-Mal) and it became empty, he would perform two units of prayer and say: "Testify that I filled you with truth and emptied you with truth!" ^2

In the next verse, He adds: "Because your Lord has inspired it" (بِأَنَّ رَبَّكَ أَوْحى‌ لَها) ^2. And the earth does not fall short in executing this command. The expression "Awha" (inspired/revealed) is used here because such mysterious speaking is contrary to the nature of the earth, and this is not possible except through a divine inspiration. Some have said: it means He inspires the earth to cast out what is within it. However, the first interpretation seems more correct and appropriate.

Then He says: "That Day, the people will depart in separate groups to be shown their deeds" (يَوْمَئِذٍ يَصْدُرُ النَّاسُ أَشْتاتاً لِيُرَوْا أَعْمالَهُمْ). "Ashtat" is the plural of "Shatt" (rhyming with shatt) meaning separate and scattered. This difference and scattering might be because the followers of every religion will enter the field of Resurrection separately, or the people of every geographical region will enter separately, or that one group will step into the Resurrection with beautiful, happy, and smiling faces, while another group will enter with sullen, dark, and gloomy faces.

Or every nation will be with their Imam, leader, and guide, as stated in Surah Al-Isra, verse 71: "The Day We shall call every people with their Imam." Or it could mean that believers will be gathered with believers and disbelievers with disbelievers. A combination of these interpretations is entirely possible as the concept of the verse is broad.

"Yasdur" is from the root "Sadr" (rhyming with sabr) meaning the departure of camels from a watering place, coming out in a mass and excited state, as opposed to "Wurud" which means their entry into the watering place. Here, it is a metaphor for the departure of various nations from their graves and their coming to the field of Resurrection for accounting.

The possibility has also been raised that it refers to their departure from the field of Resurrection and movement toward their stations in Paradise or Hell. The first meaning is more consistent with the preceding verses.

The meaning of the phrase "Liyuraw A'malahum" (to be shown their deeds) is the observation of the recompense of the deeds. Or observing the record of deeds in which every good and evil is recorded. Or an internal observation in the sense of gnosis and recognition of the nature of their deeds. Or it means the "embodiment of deeds" and the observation of the deeds themselves.

The latter interpretation is most consistent with the literal meaning of the verse, and this verse is considered one of the clearest verses regarding the issue of the embodiment of deeds—that on that day, human actions will be embodied in appropriate forms and will be present before them, and companionship with them will be a source of joy or suffering and calamity.

Then, referring to the end of the affair for each of these two groups—believer and disbeliever, righteous and wicked—He says: "So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it" (فَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ خَيْراً يَرَهُ).

"And whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it" (وَ مَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ). Again, various interpretations are mentioned here: whether one sees the recompense of the deeds, or observes the record of deeds, or the deed itself. The literal meaning of these verses is also a renewed emphasis on the issue of the "embodiment of deeds" and the observation of the deed itself, whether good or evil, on the Day of Resurrection; even if it is a needle's point of good or evil, it will be embodied before its owner and they will observe it.

"Mithqal" in the language means both weight and heaviness, as well as the scale with which the weight of objects is measured; here it carries the first meaning. For "Dharrah" (atom/speck), various interpretations have been mentioned in the language and the words of commentators: sometimes it is interpreted as a small ant, sometimes as the dust that sticks to the hand when placed on the ground and lifted, and sometimes as the very tiny particles of dust suspended in the air that become visible when sunlight shines through a hole into a dark room.

We know that today the word "Dharrah" is also applied to the "atom," and the atomic bomb is called "al-Qunbulat al-Dharriyyah." An atom is so small that it cannot be seen with the naked eye or even the most precise microscopes; only its effects are observed, and its volume and weight are measurable through scientific calculations. It is so small that several million can fit on the tip of a needle!

Whatever the concept of "Dharrah" may be, the intention here is the smallest of weights. In any case, this verse is among those that make the back tremble, showing that Allah's accounting on that day is extraordinarily precise and sensitive, and the scales for measuring deeds in the Resurrection are so delicate that they weigh and account for even the smallest of human actions.

Key Points

1. Precision and Rigor in the Accounting of the Resurrection

Not only from the final verses of this Surah but from various verses of the Quran, it is well understood that in the accounting of deeds on the Day of Resurrection, there is extraordinary precision and scrutiny. In Surah Luqman, verse 16, we read: "O my son! If it be the weight of a mustard seed and it be within a rock or in the heavens or in the earth, Allah will bring it forth (for accounting). Indeed, Allah is Subtle and Aware."

"Khardal" (mustard seed) is a very tiny seed of a well-known plant that is a proverb for smallness. These expressions show that in that great accounting, the smallest of deeds are calculated. Furthermore, these verses warn that neither small sins should be considered insignificant, nor small good deeds; whatever is subject to Divine accounting, no matter how small, is not unimportant.

Therefore, some commentators have said these verses were revealed when some of the companions of the Holy Prophet ﷺ were indifferent toward donating small amounts of wealth, saying: "Reward and recompense are given for things that we love, and small objects are not something we care for." Likewise, they were indifferent toward small sins. The verses were revealed to encourage them toward small charities and warn them against small sins.

2. Answer to a Question

A question arises here: according to these verses, man sees all his deeds in the Resurrection, whether good or evil, small or large. How is this meaning compatible with the verses of "Ihabt" (nullification), "Takfir" (expiation), "Afw" (pardon), and "Tawbah" (repentance)? For the verses of "Ihabt" say: some deeds like "disbelief" destroy all of a person's "good deeds"—"If you associate others with Allah, your work will surely become worthless" (Az-Zumar: 65)—and according to the verses of "Takfir," sometimes "good deeds" eliminate "evil deeds"—"Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds" (Hud: 114).

And the verses of "pardon" and "repentance" say: under the shadow of Divine pardon or by repenting, sins are erased. How do these concepts align with the issue of observing all good and evil deeds?

In response to this question, one point must be noted: the two principles mentioned in the above verses, which say man sees every atom of good and evil work, are in the form of a general law. We know that every law may have exceptions; the verses of "pardon," "repentance," "nullification," and "expiation" are in fact like exceptions to this general law.

Another answer is: in the case of "nullification" and "expiation," a balancing and offsetting occurs, much like "claims" and "debts" that are deducted from one another. When a person sees the result of this balance, they have in fact seen all their good and evil deeds. The same applies to "pardon" and "repentance," for pardon does not occur without merit and worthiness, and repentance itself is one of the good deeds.

Some have given another answer here which does not seem correct: that disbelievers see the result of their good deeds in this world, just as believers see the punishment for their bad deeds in this world. But it appears that the verses under discussion relate to the Resurrection, not the world. Furthermore, it is not a universal rule that every believer and disbeliever sees the result of their deeds in this world.

3. The Most Comprehensive Verses of the Quran

It is narrated from Abdullah ibn Mas'ud that the most "Muhkam" (firm/decisive) verses of the Holy Quran are these very verses: "So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it." He used to refer to them as "Al-Jami'ah" (The Comprehensive). Truly, deep faith in its content is enough to set a person on the path of truth and restrain them from any kind of evil and corruption. Therefore, it is mentioned in a hadith that a man came to the Holy Prophet ﷺ and said: "Teach me from what Allah has taught you."

The Holy Prophet ﷺ entrusted him to one of his companions to teach him the Quran, and he taught him Surah "Idha Zulzilat al-Ard" to the end. The man stood up and said: "This is enough for me" (and in another narration: "This one verse suffices me")!

The Holy Prophet ﷺ said: "Leave him be, for he has become a Faqih (one with deep understanding)!" (And according to one narration, he said: "He returned as a Faqih!"). The reason is clear, for one who knows that our deeds, even to the size of an "atom" or a "mustard seed," are subject to accounting, will occupy himself today with his own accounting, and this has the greatest educational effect on him [^1]. Nevertheless, in another narration from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, it is stated that when the verse "So whoever does..." was revealed, I said: "O Messenger of Allah, will I see all my deeds?" He said: "Yes." I said: "Those big ones?" He said: "Yes." I said: "The very small ones?" He said: "Yes." I said: "O woe to me! May my mother sit and mourn for me!" He said: "Glad tidings to you, O Abu Sa'id! For 'good deeds' are counted tenfold, up to seven hundred times, and Allah multiplies it for whomever He wills. But every sin is punished as one sin, or Allah pardons. And know that no one will be saved by his deeds alone! (Unless Allah's grace encompasses him)." I said: "Even you, O Messenger of Allah?!" He said: "Even I, unless Allah encompasses me with His mercy!" ^2

O Allah! Where Your Prophet with such greatness relies only on Your pardon, our situation is clear.

[^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 495; the same theme appears in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 650. ^2: Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 381.

O Lord! If our deeds be the criterion for our salvation, woe unto us; and if Your grace be our helper, how blessed are we?! O God! On that day when all deeds, small and large, are embodied before us, we look only to Your all-encompassing grace.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Az-Zalzalah.
